data.title: Carl Zeiss Meditec: Why This Company Is A Good Choice For 2026
data.description:
Some investments that I make take a long time to mature. Some even take years - and if you're a value investor like I am, you have to kind of decide whether you'll allow companies to take as long as that to "mature". I argue that if you're unwilling to take this time, you should probably consider not using a value approach for your investments. It simply involves too much risk. The company I'm going to review is a perfect example of a business that has taken a very long time to mature (and is still far from there), but where the quality isn't anything that I am worried about in terms of the investment.

My last article on the company can be found here - a negative development of about 4% compared to a positive from the SPY of about 6%. Not a huge difference, but if you look at the longer-term performance difference, there's a significant variation with the company significantly underperforming indices.

This is therefore a position that is in the negative, and where I believe it will take some time to see recovery. Does that worry me?

Not really.

The reason why it doesn't bother or worry me as much as perhaps some other companies do is because the underlying fundamentals for the company are very solid. Recent results, which I will go into here, confirm this, and we are therefore seeing a negative correlation, i.e., the company is going down despite results going up. Whenever this happens, that can be a very good indicator of some interesting valuation potentials - and is why I'm excited to see the valuation at this time, even more so than before.

We're in a very inflated market overall. It's not wrong to characterize the market as 'overvalued', something I have done in previous pieces. I believe a company like this one is poised to outperform over the medium to longer term, provided that these positive trends continue.

Let's see what we have here.

Carl Zeiss Meditec: Recent Results Confirm My Upside And Considerations

The company's recent results are about a month old at this time, but I am surprised that we haven't seen more of a positive reaction as a result of them than we have here. Why?

Because Carl Zeiss Meditec achieved over €2.2B worth of revenue, an increase of over 7.5% YoY in this still volatile market, even higher at a constant currency and adjusted for M&A effects. EBITA was up as well - in line with the company's forecast and guidance, and the order backlog amounted to well over €350M, which is high for the company.

More importantly, Carl Zeiss noted a significant increase in order flows and market recovery.

Perhaps I would be less positive if the company's operations and business was concerning some less specialized, more commoditized sector. But this is not the case. The company is in a highly narrow segment, and owns some of the most attractive technologies and patents in that segment. As you can see above, the company has specialized to covert the entire lifecycle for its main treatment area, and due to increased average ages, we're likely to see increases in procedures.

The company's secondary segment, Microsurgery, is about 23% of total revenue for the full year, and is still growing. Furthermore, remember that almost 60% of shares are held not by market or float, but by Carl Zeiss AG, which makes foreign control or influence of this company, as of this time, a non-issue. No one on the broader market can affect what the company does, because a majority shareholding is private.

The company reached multiple milestones as of the full-year results. This includes a recurring revenue of 50% or above, which means that the company is now more and more insulated from its unit sales. This is a strong indicator that the company's earnings/AEPS should be treated with perhaps a bit more adherence to the historical premium, because forward volatility is likely to be lower. Higher percentages of service revenue tend to have that effect, after all.

The company's risks that remain are related to exposure - too much of the company's business is still in China at 25%, which has been a drag on revenues and profits, and a source of the volatility that has been driving this company down. At the same time, other APAC geographies are going well.

I, personally, believe that the company's prospects for China are bleak. With a weaker consumer climate and higher economic uncertainty in China, I find it unlikely that consumers (or commercial players) will show the same interest in the company's SMILE products and machines, which, while different than LASIK and offering higher margins, are also significantly more expensive. I would consider any forecast for the company based on a full China recovery as too exuberant.

Basically, a positive view on Carl Zeiss as of the end of the year is possible if you believe in the megatrends mentioned, in new product appeal, in new market sales and better margins (ex-China), and with good integration of companies like DORC, which is already contributing to revenues and results. I do not think it's wrong to say that competitors are less prepared than Carl Zeiss. None of them, Topcon, B+L, Alcon, or Haag-Streit, offer the breadth of services, such as diagnostics, imaging, refractive lasers, or vitrectomies, that this company does. Carl Zeiss is the only one with full vertical integration - and vertical integration is, in my view, a source of competitiveness and strength, as we saw, among other things, during COVID-19, which has been researched into since.

So, overall, I do not see why the company should trade negatively, or as cheaply as it does today. The current valuation puts Carl Zeiss at 23.8x P/E on average, but this needs to be put into the context of a 10-15 year history, which puts the company closer to 40x P/E. I have said in previous articles that this is one of the companies I allow some of the most premium in, and I stand by that.

Given the current growth estimates, even if they turn out 10-20% lower than estimated, the upside for this company is non-trivial.

Valuation For Carl Zeiss Meditec: I'm Now Buying More

Historically, it's often been between Carl Zeiss Meditec and peers like Healthineers (SEMHF). However, as of January 2026, I believe the advantage is skewed towards Carl Zeiss.

Even just a forecast to 30x P/E, which I have historically used as a bit of an indicator (and which is also the 20-year average, by the way), gives us an upside in the triple digits until 2028E.

Even discounted further, say to 20-21x P/E, lower than I believe this company has any right to be, the relative upside for the company is significant and worth considering. The one disadvantage for the business is the very low dividend yield. At this particular time, it's no more than 1.3%, despite the low valuation, and this means that your dividend returns or amounts are never really going to amount to (comparatively) much here.

Instead, focus on capital appreciation for the company, and focus on what you can get that way. An argument for Siemens Healthineers above Carl Zeiss Meditec is a higher yield, solid credit rating, and a more stable overall potential upside - but by comparison, Siemens Healthineers has an upside that's about 8-10% lower per year than Carl Zeiss.

My arguments for investing in this company remain. The company is a market leader with a moat, I believe is second to very few companies. It has a focus on innovation, proven by history. It has a strong financial situation, with extremely low overall debt, and above this has a very strong valuation and upside. I would say it's fair to characterize the company as "cheap" here, which means that it fulfills all of my investment criteria for 2026E.

It's also why I have decided to expand my position in Carl Zeiss Meditec, and why it's my number #1 pick in the sector as it stands today. I believe what headwinds there are, are mostly short-term in nature, and while the Chinese market is tricky, the risk I would instead focus on is geopolitical volatility - meaning tariffs.

But I believe even those will only take "so much" from the company.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risk to Carl Zeiss is a lengthened price/mix or market issue, as the company has been experiencing on and off for some time here. If this were to extend to longer periods, there would come a point where I would have to impact the company's fair value. As I've stated before, companies growing at much lower rates should not be valued at 30x+ P/E, especially not with a sub-2% yield. But that's not where we are; Carl Zeiss is instead a very strong grower of its revenues and earnings.

Secondary and tertiary risks (the ones that I would focus on here) are more related to tariffs. Given the company's large amount of exposure to NA and the current tariff situation (even though at the time of writing, the existence of the tariffs is in question, given that it's a question for the Supreme Court in the USA), impacts here could be significant. Even with perhaps more clarity here, I believe this still poses a significant risk. Even with the tariffs as a risk, I view the company to be attractive well beyond its current price, with a significant upside potential.

I maintain that the company is a "BUY" here and attractive. I also maintain that it fulfills all of my investment criteria and is therefore worth your attention.

Thesis

Carl Zeiss Meditec is a qualitative medtech business originating in Germany but with an international growth and sales profile. It does not offer a compelling yield, but what it does offer is a decently compelling overall long-term upside, even after the 2025A that I am presenting here. Even conservatively speaking, if you accept some of the premiumization here, you have triple-digit upside potential, with 35% and potentially as high as 40% per year as of the end of the year 2027, if you're willing to put the company above 30x P/E. For safety, I would probably limit myself to about 30x - but higher returns have been possible historically, and technically still are.

As mentioned in previous pieces, these assumptions require you to accept a very decent premium. Nonetheless, I continue to say "buy" (a strong one now) when it comes to this company. A quality company has the right to cost quite a bit - and this is the highest I accept for a business like this. I believe the market is severely undervaluing the upside potential of this stock.

My fair value target is now, as of September 2025, a 30x-33x forward P/E, potentially and realistically upwards of 36x. The investment PT is now at €85/share, for a 15%+ annualized upside to this fair value target. I maintain my rating to "Strong Buy" as of this article, and I don't change my PT, despite the lower share price.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative, and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 2 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 5/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this and a valuation target of €85/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Strong Buy" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: WEEK: Yield From Weekly Distributions
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

Investors seeking exposure to the treasury bill market have several options for doing so in an indexed manner. The Roundhill Weekly T-Bill ETF (WEEK) is one such option, with the particular feature of offering optimization of the payment schedule for the bills it purchases. As the name of the ETF suggests, the fund sacrifices maximization of returns in order to provide weekly payments to its investors, thereby optimizing the aforementioned payment schedule. It is important to make this clear before moving on to the thesis of this analysis, as it will depend greatly on the purposes of each investor. Readers should consider how they manage their liquidity and what their purposes are in managing their cash flow.

As we will see later, the US government is financing itself largely through Treasury bills (short-term bonds). If the government issues more supply, this means that bills become marginally cheaper and short-term yields rise. This is positive for funds that trade in bills, as they are short-term instruments and their interest income (carry) adjusts quickly. As regular readers will know, I generally recommend managing surplus liquidity by investing in this type of short-term instrument. For investors who value weekly cash flow, I recommend buying this vehicle, as there are no other market alternatives that settle carry on a weekly basis (we will look at the drawbacks later). If your goal is to maximize the return on that invested liquidity, then I recommend selling the vehicle and exposing yourself to other alternatives that are more suitable for that purpose, which we will analyze below.

ETF Overview

Looking at the main characteristics of this ETF, we find that it has a 30-day security yield of 3.58% with weekly distribution and a distribution ratio of 3.51%. This in itself does not tell us much, but if we compare it with the current yield on 3-month Treasury bills (see image below), we see that the difference is 0.13% in terms of the distribution rate. Here we can see the effect of the fund's fees (expense ratio of 0.19%) and the bid/ask spread of 0.03%.

If we look at the fund's holdings, we see that all of its assets are invested in treasury bills with different maturities. Each bill in its portfolio has practically the same weight (7.60% - 7.80%).

The Peers

When analyzing the drawbacks of WEEK and proposing some alternatives for investors seeking weekly income distribution, we will focus on WEEK's most similar peers in the market. For this comparison, I have selected the ETFs SGOV, BIL, USFR, and TFLO (the latter two do not purchase bills, but rather have FRNs attached to short-term rates in their portfolios). As can be seen in the first image, in terms of fees, WEEK does not compete with SGOV or BIL (funds in the same category). These have annual expense ratios of 0.09% and 0.14%, respectively. As I said earlier, if you want weekly payments, buy WEEK, but if that doesn't matter so much to you, I would stick with SGOV to park liquidity. In terms of AUM, we can see that WEEK has significantly lower assets under management than SGOV or BIL. This may directly influence a wider bid-ask spread in the case of WEEK, with less market depth.

When we look at the images of price and total returns, we see the following. WEEK is much less “volatile” than other ETFs and performs worse in terms of total returns. 3.47% vs. 4.21% for SGOV. This shows that WEEK's weekly payment management sacrifices some performance. With a higher cost, I still recommend SGOV, if you don't mind not receiving those weekly flows.

T- Bill Treasury Management

Finally, I will focus on the more macroeconomic part of the article and on the statement I have made about why I prefer Treasury bills for managing short-term liquidity. The two images above show the percentage of US sovereign debt issuance relative to the country's GDP throughout the 21st century (first image) and the total percentage of debt issuance by instrument (image 2). As the reader can see, the US Treasury has been issuing Treasury bills throughout all these years until it is now financed mostly by this type of short-term instrument. Specifically, in 2025, most of the issuance by maturity is bills. This directly influences the Treasury bills themselves, lowering their prices and raising their yields. If we add to this a Treasury and a Federal Reserve as structural demanders of bills ( see RMP program), we can say that a Treasury loaded with bills tends to sustain attractive yields at the front end as long as the Fed does not cut aggressively. That is why I like to manage my liquidity with Treasury bills in this macroeconomic environment of slow rate cuts. Thank you for reading.
